From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f4/Cap-binding_complex_1H2T.png/250px-Cap-binding_complex_1H2T.png)](/wiki/File:Cap-binding_complex_1H2T.png)

Surface model of the cap-binding complex (heavy chain pink, light chain yellow, m7G and GDP as balls), after PDB 1H2T.

Formation on 5' ends of mRNAs

The [5' cap](/wiki/5%27_cap "5' cap") of eukaryotic [messenger RNA](/wiki/Messenger_RNA "Messenger RNA") is bound at all times by various **cap-binding complexes** (**CBCs**).

## Nuclear cap-binding complex

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cap_binding_complex&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Nuclear cap-binding complex")]

In the nucleus, freshly transcribed mRNA molecules are bound on the 5' cap by the [nuclear cap-binding complex](/wiki/Nuclear_cap-binding_protein_complex "Nuclear cap-binding protein complex") of Cbc1/Cbc2 in yeast or CBP20/CBP80 in metazoans. These aid in the export of the mRNA and protect it from [decapping](/wiki/Messenger_RNA_decapping "Messenger RNA decapping"). They also serve as a marker for the so-called pioneer round of translation when the message is examined by [nonsense mediated decay](/wiki/Nonsense_mediated_decay "Nonsense mediated decay").[[1]](#cite_note-Isken2007-1)

## Cytoplasmic cap-binding complex

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cap_binding_complex&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Cytoplasmic cap-binding complex")]

After the first round of translation ("pioneer round"), CBC20/80 is replaced by the [translation](/wiki/Translation_(genetics) "Translation (genetics)") initiation factor [eIF4E](/wiki/EIF4E "EIF4E").[[2]](#cite_note-2) The eIF4F complex (eIF4E, [eIF4G](/wiki/EIF4G "EIF4G") and [eIF4A](/wiki/EIF4A "EIF4A")) then regulates translation in response to the state of the cell via its [phosphorylation](/wiki/Phosphorylation "Phosphorylation") state and again protects the message from decapping.[[3]](#cite_note-Gross2003-3)

## Decapping complex

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cap_binding_complex&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Decapping complex")]

When translationally repressed or marked for decay by various mechanisms the 5' cap is bound by the mRNA decapping enzyme [DCP2](/wiki/DCP2 "DCP2"). A host of proteins accompany it including [UPF1](/wiki/UPF1 "UPF1"), [UPF2](/wiki/UPF2 "UPF2"), [UPF3A](/wiki/UPF3A "UPF3A"), Dcp1, Dhh1, [XRN1](/wiki/XRN1 "XRN1"), and others. The decapping enzyme removes the 5' cap leading to destruction of the message.[[4]](#cite_note-Parker2007-4)
